userMessage: do your best i have MSO6B 4 channels

response.text:
Given your MSO6B with 4 analog channels, the best practical plan is to treat this as two separate captures: CH1 for the 10 Gb/s eye, and CH2/CH3/B1 for the CAN-ground-bounce event.

For the 10 Gb/s eye on CH1:
- Bit period is about 100 ps, so start with 200 ps/div to 500 ps/div.
- Use Sample mode first; turn on FastAcq and use the Temperature palette for density grading.
- Use the highest sample rate you can sustain and a moderate-to-long record length so the eye builds quickly without killing update rate.
- Use full analog bandwidth if you care about jitter, edge speed, and eye closure; if the trace is too noisy, step bandwidth down one notch to improve SNR.
- For a true recovered-clock eye, use the scope’s serial-analysis/CDR feature if licensed; that exact recovered-clock SCPI path was not verified in this turn, so I would not automate that part blindly.
- The “capture 10 seconds” part is the main caveat: a literal 10-second raw 10 Gb/s waveform at full-bandwidth scope sample rates is usually not realistic as one continuous record. The realistic approach is 10 seconds of eye accumulation/persistence, then save a representative waveform and screenshot.

What to look for in the eye:
- Eye height: vertical opening margin.
- Eye width: horizontal timing margin.
- Crossing percentage: should be stable and near expected symmetry.
- Total jitter and separation of random vs deterministic jitter if available.
- Rise/fall asymmetry.
- Overshoot, undershoot, and ringing.
- Closure caused by ISI, equalization mismatch, or bandwidth limiting.
- If mask testing is available, mask hits are the fastest pass/fail indicator.

For the CAN/ground-bounce setup on CH2/CH3/B1:
- Put CAN on CH2 and configure B1 as CAN decode sourced from CH2.
- Show the B1 decode overlay.
- Use A trigger as an edge trigger on CH2, falling edge, with level at the dominant-bit transition midpoint.
- Enable B trigger, set it to occur by time, and set the B delay to 200 ns after A.
- Make B trigger type Runt on CH3 so the bounce is the actual qualification event.
- Turn on FastFrame and set 500 frames.
- Use sequence/stop-after acquisition so the scope fills the 500 frames and stops.
- Query the number of frames acquired to confirm completion.

The verified pieces from this turn that are safe conceptually are:
- BUS:B1:TYPe CAN
- BUS:B1:CAN:SOUrce ...
- DISplay:WAVEView1:BUS:B1:STATE ON
- TRIGger:A:TYPe EDGE
- TRIGger:A:EDGE:SOUrce CH2
- TRIGger:A:EDGE:SLOpe FALL
- TRIGger:B:STATE ON
- TRIGger:B:BY TIMe
- TRIGger:B:TIMe 200e-9
- TRIGger:B:TYPe RUNT
- TRIGger:B:RUNT:SOUrce CH3
- HORizontal:FASTframe:STATE ON
- HORizontal:FASTframe:COUNt 500
- ACQuire:NUMFRAMESACQuired?

The pieces that are still not verified enough to automate safely are:
- Exact recovered-clock trigger/CDR SCPI for the 10 Gb/s eye.
- Exact runt-threshold SCPI headers for CH3 bounce qualification.
- Exact FastFrame timestamp query/export command.
- A trustworthy CH bandwidth SCPI record; the lookup for that one came back inconsistent.

My recommendation:
- Automate the verified skeleton first.
- Set the runt thresholds and recovered-clock eye mode from the front panel once.
- Save CH2 and CH3 waveforms plus a screenshot.
- In the Python correlation step, line up:
  1) frame index,
  2) A-event reference time,
  3) B-event detection time near A + 200 ns,
  4) CAN decode text from B1,
  5) CH3 bounce amplitude/width per frame.

If you want, I can next give you a clean operator checklist for front-panel setup on the MSO6B using only the verified parts and clearly mark the few manual-only items.
